critique.summary: The article announces the release of Kitten TTS v0.8, an open-source, lightweight text-to-speech library with models ranging from 15M to 80M parameters and sizes between 25 MB and 80 MB. It highlights the library's CPU-optimized performance, multiple built-in voices, and features such as adjustable speech speed and text preprocessing.
mainPoints: Kitten TTS v0.8 offers three model sizes: 15M (nano), 40M (micro), and 80M (mini) parameters.
Models range in size from 25 MB (int8 quantized nano model) to 80 MB, suitable for edge deployment without requiring a GPU.
The library supports 8 built-in voices and outputs high-quality 24 kHz audio.
It is CPU-optimized using ONNX for efficient inference.
The project is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and provides commercial support options.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Kitten TTS are consistent with publicly available information on the official GitHub repository and related documentation. The model sizes, parameter counts, and features such as CPU optimization and multiple voices are accurately described. The project is indeed open-source and uses ONNX for inference. The mention of commercial support and the license type also align with the repository details. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: User experience and audio quality comparisons with other TTS models are not discussed.
Potential limitations or issues, such as those reported with the int8 quantized nano model, are briefly mentioned but not elaborated.
The article does not provide independent benchmarks or third-party reviews of the model's performance.
Compatibility with various operating systems and integration complexity for developers is only briefly mentioned.
The roadmap and future development plans could impact the stability and features of the library.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the new Kitten TTS models, focusing on their size, quality, and usability. Commenters inquire about technical details, performance, expressive control, platform compatibility, and licensing options.

comments.author: ks2048
comments.text:
There's a number of recent, good quality, small TTS models.

If the author doesn't describe some detail about the data, training, or a novel architecture, etc, I only assume they just took another one, do a little finetuning, and repackage as a new product.
comments.analysis: This comment critically highlights the lack of technical detail which is important for assessing novelty and contribution, making it a valid and insightful observation.

comments.author: kevin42
comments.text:
What I love about OpenClaw is that I was able to send it a message on Discord with just this github URL and it started sending me voice messages using it within a few minutes. It also gave me a bunch of different benchmarks and sample audio.

I'm impressed with the quality given the size. I don't love the voices, but it's not bad. Running on an intel 9700 CPU, it's about 1.5x realtime using the 80M model. It wasn't any faster running on a 3080 GPU though.
comments.analysis: Provides practical user experience and performance benchmarks, which are useful for evaluating the model's real-world usability.

comments.author: altruios
comments.text:
One of the core features I look for is expressive control.

Either in the form of the api via pitch/speed/volume controls, for more deterministic controls.

Or in expressive tags such as [coughs], [urgently], or [laughs in melodic ascending and descending arpeggiated gibberish babbles].

the 25MB model is amazingly good for being 25MB. How does it handle expressive tags?
comments.analysis: Raises an important feature inquiry about expressive control, which is relevant for TTS quality and user customization.

comments.author: ks2048
comments.text: You should put examples comparing the 4 models you released - same text spoken by each.
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion to provide comparative examples, which would help users evaluate differences between models.

comments.author: ilaksh
comments.text:
Thanks for open sourcing this.

Is there any way to do a custom voice as a DIY? Or we need to go through you? If so, would you consider making a pricing page for purchasing a license/alternative voice? All but one of the voices are unusable in a business context.
comments.analysis: Asks relevant questions about customization and licensing, highlighting practical concerns for business use.

comments.author: great_psy
comments.text:
Thanks for working on this!

Is there any way to get those running on iPhone ? I would love to have the ability for it to read articles to me like a podcast.
comments.analysis: Expresses appreciation and asks about platform compatibility, a common practical concern.

comments.author: fwsgonzo
comments.text:
How much work would it be to use the C++ ONNX run-time with this instead of Python? Is it a Claudeable amount of work?

The iOS version is Swift-based.
comments.analysis: Inquires about technical integration and runtime environment, though the term 'Claudeable' is unclear and may reduce clarity.

comments.author: Tacite
comments.analysis: A brief but relevant question about language support, important for assessing model applicability.
